data.title: Amazon's Next Big Profit Driver
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) has surged past the $220 target since my last coverage, delivering a +25% return — more than double the S&P 500’s gain. The company’s strength lies in its ability to drive revenue growth while boosting profitability, with its high-margin ad business and record third-party sales fueling expansion. Prime membership growth, logistics optimizations, and automation are cutting costs and reinforcing its competitive edge. With e-commerce dominance and multiple growth levers, Amazon remains a compelling long-term bet, making any pullbacks a buying opportunity.

Stronger Top-line With Profitability Uplifts Possible in CY25

Amazon generated $187.8B in revenue for Q,4-24, marking a 10% YoY increase. This growth is vital under the $700M FX headwind that impacted the quarter. Without this headwind, top-line growth would have been 11%, exceeding the top end of the company’s guidance. This indicates that the company can grow top-line despite adverse FX movements through the strength of its diversified business model. Here, Amazon’s ad business continues to be a growth yielder. The ad business has derived $17.3B in revenue in Q4 with an 18% YoY jump outpacing the consolidated growth, equating to an annual revenue run rate of $69B, 2X the $29B reported four years ago. Sponsored products account for the largest portion of ad revenue, and Amazon sees further growth opportunities in this segment along with the retail business.

Amazon’s Stores business (North America and International segments) had solid growth. The North America segment derived a 10% YoY top-line jump. Whereas the international segment grew by 9% if we exclude FX impacts. This growth was based on Amazon’s moves to expand its product selection pool, lower prices, and improve user convenience. The company’s platform added brands like Clinique, Estee Lauder (EL), Oura Rings, and Armani Beauty. This boosts Amazon.com’s product assortment and attracts a larger customer base.

Third-party sellers now accounted for 61% of the items sold on Amazon in CY24. This is the highest annual mix ever. This shift towards a higher proportion of third-party sales is a boost for Amazon. Why? It reduces the company’s inventory risk and capital requirements and increases the platform’s attractiveness due to its vast selection. The launch of Amazon Haul in Q4 (which offers ultra-low-priced products in one convenient destination) has also been well-received and has led to strong unit growth that outpaced top-line growth.

Further, Amazon’s pricing strategy continues to be a vital advantage. In Q4, consumers saved over $15B through Amazon’s low everyday prices and promotional events (like Prime Big Deal Days, Black Friday, and Cyber Monday). As per the Federal annual pricing study, Amazon had the lowest online prices for the eighth consecutive year. It is averaging 14% lower than other leading US retailers. This pricing advantage plus fast and reliable delivery reinforces Amazon’s lead in the market.

Moving to Amazon Prime, in CY24, Amazon expanded its same-day delivery sites by over 60%, now serving over 140 metro areas. The company delivered +9B units the same or the next day worldwide (setting a new record). This focus on delivery speed and convenience is driving accelerated growth in Prime membership and may continue to push up Amazon’s top-line growth.

As of now, Amazon Prime provides comprehensive benefits for $14.99/month. With that, Prime members can have unlimited free shipping on 300M items (often with same-day/one-day delivery) and access to exclusive shopping events (like Prime Day), premium programming and live sports (Prime Video), ad-free listening of 100M songs and podcasts with Amazon Music, and unlimited grocery delivery from Whole Foods Market and Amazon Fresh (for $9.99/month). Also, Prime members benefit from a free Grubhub+ membership and a $0.10/gallon fuel discount at BP, Amoco, and AMPM stations. So, in short, the value proposition of Prime is compelling against competitor membership services that offer fewer benefits at higher prices.

At the bottom line, the regionalization of the US fulfillment network and the redesign of the inbound network have improved inventory placement (bringing more items closer to customers). Ahead of Black Friday, Amazon improved the percentage of ordered units available in the ideal building by over 40% YoY. This optimization reduces delivery times and lowers expenses. The company has also optimized the number of items sent in the same package (reducing packaging waste and transportation costs). Per-unit transportation costs continue to decline as Amazon builds out and optimizes its last-mile network. These base efficiencies have led to a reduction in the global cost of serving on a per-unit basis for the second consecutive year.

Looking ahead to CY25, Amazon may continue to reduce costs through better inventory placement, expansion of the same-day delivery network, and increased use of robotics + automation. These may derive continued improvements in operating margins under an expanding top line. The street is also expecting continued similar top-line growth in CY25 and CY26. In short, revenue expansion is becoming more profitable, and this will benefit the stock’s valuations in the mid-term. Now, considering the top-line valuation and growth premiums over the sector median, the stock is fairly valued, and any correction in the stock may serve as an entry point for bulls.

Amazon’s Growing Risks: Logistics, Automation, and Ad Growth Under Pressure

Amazon’s dependence on third-party logistics partners (particularly UPS) presents a business-side risk. However, Amazon has increasingly shifted its shipments to its own logistics network to reduce its dependence. This move has allowed Amazon to scale up its operations with control over delivery speed (and cost). UPS has decided to exit from 50% of the volume it previously handled for Amazon due to lower margins. This transition places additional pressure on Amazon’s internal logistics capabilities. It requires the company to handle a larger share of its shipments independently. Amazon has managed this shift so far, but any disruptions in its logistics network (due to capacity, labor issues, or operational inefficiencies) can hit hard on delivery performance.

Also, Amazon’s investment in robotics and automation has seen cost savings and productivity improvements, but scaling the tech across its massive fulfillment network presents a scalability issue. The recent integration of new robotics initiatives in the Shreveport facility has shown positives, but it is still in the early stages. Expanding the upgrades to other facilities may require massive time and capital investment. Any delays or failures in the deployment of robotics could cap the reduction of its cost to serve and delivery speeds.

Finally, Amazon’s ad business is not without its issues. The ad market is highly competitive, with major leads by Google (GOOG), Facebook (META), and other emerging platforms drilling for market share. For Amazon to build its ad business, top-line growth depends on its level of investment in order to differentiate its ad portfolio. The company has led in full-funnel ads and leveraging client data for targeted campaigns, but it faces increasing scrutiny over data privacy. Any shift in data privacy regulations towards targeted ads can crack Amazon’s sharpness in monetizing its ad platform. Lastly, as the ad business grows on an increasingly large base, maintaining high growth rates will become more challenging and may lead to slower top-line ad growth. This can cause sharp hits on the stock.

Takeaway

Amazon continues to dominate e-commerce while expanding its high-margin ad business and optimizing logistics for profitability. Its strategic investments in Prime, automation, and supply chain efficiency position it for sustained growth. While scaling challenges and competition pose risks, Amazon’s ability to execute on its strengths makes it a compelling long-term opportunity.

data.title: Birkenstock Adds Winter Assortment, But The Stock Remains Too Expensive
data.description:
Birkenstock Holding plc (NYSE:BIRK) reported Q1 2025 results, with revenue rising 19% year-over-year.

Still, the market reacted negatively as the company maintained its full-year guidance at 15%-17% growth, signaling a potential deceleration from 20% last year.

Another concern was the composition of growth: wholesale outperformed (+30%) while DTC slowed to +10% (vs. +30% last year). Additionally, wholesale expansion was largely driven by increased assortment at existing doors (90% of growth), raising concerns about saturation.

On the positive, there were good signs of product diversification—closed-toe silhouettes accounted for 50% of revenue this quarter.

Still, the company’s valuation remains extreme at 27x next-twelve-months (NTM) earnings, considering the large fashion risk and revenue deceleration signs.

Recap: a great brand, but high fashion risk

My original Birkenstock article is from July 2024, and I rated it a Hold. Back then, I considered the company to have a strong franchise, but it was nonetheless largely a single-product, high-fashion risk brand. Trading at nearly 30x earnings, it required flawless execution and sustained high growth to justify its valuation. So far, the stock is down despite posting 20/15% growth, giving some credit to my concerns.

Q1 2025 results

Strong growth but decelerating and lower quality?: Revenues were up 19% YOY in the company's slowest quarter (not that many people buy sandals during the Northern Hemisphere winter).

This should be a good figure, but there are also some more concerning points regarding growth quality.

First, the company maintained a 15/17% yearly guidance, below last year (20%) and below 1Q25. Second, DTC was significantly slower than the whole company, at +10% YOY (management said the comp was hard in 1Q24 at 30% YOY).

Third, wholesale was the main driver (+30%), which is generally good, but at least 90% of growth (acc. to management) came from assorting the same doors. This is not bad, but it may not be something that is repeatable, given Birkenstock is expanding towards winter-oriented sales mostly. Management said that wholesale and DTC growth rates should meet across the year.

Management claims to maintain strict assortment discipline, citing a 40% sell-through increase. However, this is somewhat at odds with the slowdown in DTC growth.

Very good signs of product diversification: A notable highlight from the quarter was Birkenstock’s growing success in diversifying its product mix beyond sandals. This was the main risk I was concerned with since I started covering the name.

Closed-toe silhouettes made up 50% of revenue in 1Q25. In part, this was driven by assortment expansion at wholesalers, and also by the winter in the Northern Hemisphere, but still a strong signal that the brand is moving beyond its traditional image.

Of the 600 basis points gained by closed-toe products, 300bps came from lace-up shoes and boots, marking a potentially significant expansion into a new category. While it remains to be seen whether this trend will be sustained throughout the year, it is a step in the right direction.

Opening in Asia: Asia grew 47% YOY, adding 3/4% of sales growth (it represents 13% of sales versus 10% last year). Here, wholesale is also key, with mono-brand partner stores. Still a small market, but that could easily represent another Europe or America.

Valuation remains too optimistic

Birkenstock Holding plc stock remains priced for perfection.

Based on the company’s full-year guidance, revenues should be $2.3 billion (+15%-17% FX-neutral growth). Adjusted EBITDA margin was guided at 31%, resulting in adjusted EBITDA of $713 million. Removing $100 million in D&A and $100 million in interest, plus 25% in taxes, results in FY25 net income of $387 million.

Compared with a market cap of $10.5 billion, this is a P/E ratio of 27x. Such a P/E requires A LOT of growth to make sense, which Birkenstock could achieve, but the problem is that the price already discounts it. For example, even if Birkenstock sustains 15% annual growth for six years without taking on new debt, its valuation in 2030 would still be ~13x earnings.

The positive future is already discounted, and the risks (deceleration, fashion risk, consumer discretionary) are ignored.

BIRK is the case of a great company but not a great stock. I maintain my Hold on the name.

data.title: Palantir: The Math Doesn't Work For Me
data.description:
Description

Palantir Technologies (NASDAQ:PLTR) was founded in May 2003 by Alex Karp and Peter Thiel, among others. Some of the first investors included the U.S. Central Intelligence Agency and Peter Thiel's venture capital firm, Founders Fund. From its beginnings, Palantir has been intimately connected with some of the most powerful intelligence agencies in the Western world. As discussed by Alex Karp in some interviews, the firm has been playing a critical role not only in the conflict between Ukraine and Russia but pretty much all around the world, including in the Middle East and Taiwan. The fact that Alex Karp was the first Western CEO to visit Volodymyr Zelensky after Russia's invasion of Ukraine in February 2022 illustrates the firm's mission-critical offerings.

The deteriorating conditions in the Middle East are slowly putting the Strait of Hormuz into focus. Even though this route is absolutely vital for oil trade, it is less so for containership. In my view, the real deal when it comes to geopolitical risk is the Taiwan Strait, and if Hormuz is the world's jugular vein for oil, the Taiwan Strait and the South China Sea are the jugulars for containerships. Danaos Corporation: Providing A Long-Volatility Exposure To Geopolitics

Palantir is at the intersection of artificial intelligence and geopolitics, two of the most powerful trends affecting the world today. The AI theme has been one of the most impactful on equity markets over the last quarters, and Palantir has been one of the key beneficiaries in this regard. When it comes to geopolitics, tensions between China and Taiwan continue to increase. For example, China simulated a sea and air blockade of Taiwan in October 2024, an additional sign of mounting tensions in the region. With an ongoing AI race and an increasingly unstable world from a geopolitical perspective, the current euphoria reflected by the stock's meteoric rise is understandable in a way. How many companies can pretend to sit at the intersection point between some of the most powerful forces shaping Western civilization today?

Before discussing valuation, I want to acknowledge the validity of the optimism surrounding the company. However, from a psychological point of view, one risk associated with optimism is that it becomes detached from reality and morphs into euphoria. Looking back on my personal journey from downtown Toronto to a yurt in the woods, I realize that sustained optimism, kept in check by real and reasonable challenges, is the strategy that I unconsciously adopted. If my journey would have been characterized by bouts of euphoria followed by major disappointment, I don't think I would have made it. In a sense, the euphoria of today is simply the polar opposite of the panic of tomorrow. How long before there is a shift in polarity? One thing I have learned from people suffering from bipolar disorder is that it can happen extremely quickly following a completely unexpected trigger. If stock prices are ruled by sentiment in the short term, I think psychology may offer some useful guidelines.

Palantir may also be one of the most well-connected companies in the world. It is now well-known that Palantir, Peter Thiel, and multiple intelligence agencies have had close relationships for many years and even decades in some cases.

In turn, Peter Thiel was a key fund provider for Trump's election campaign. Inside the Trump administration, the link between Peter Thiel, Elon Musk, and David Sacks is also apparent as they are part of the infamous PayPal Mafia. As Forbes mentioned in July 2024, the relationship between Thiel and JD Vance, the 50th Vice President of the United States under the Trump administration, goes back to 2011. Moreover, there probably was not another time in history when the CEO of a major corporation was so close publicly with a President. From this perspective, the relationship between Elon Musk and Donald Trump is quite unique.

Palantir was listed on the NYSE at $10.00 per share in September 2020. Based on the 2.17 billion diluted shares outstanding at the time, it was equivalent to a market capitalization of ~$21.7 billion. In contrast, Palantir was valued at $20 billion in December 2015 following a $880 million funding round. Until May 2023, Palantir's stock was still trading below the $10.00 threshold. In fact, it was only when Trump's election campaign started to gain momentum in the summer of 2024 that the share price took off and went parabolic. With 2.5 billion fully diluted shares outstanding and a price of around $110.00 per share, we are now talking about a market capitalization of ~$275 billion. Despite generating less than $3.0 billion in revenues over the course of 2024, Palantir is now the 31st largest component in the S&P 500, with a weighting of 0.47%. When the company was added to the S&P 500 towards the end of September 2024, the share price was still hovering around $35.00.

As discussed in a previous article about MicroStrategy (MSTR), large inflows from passive funds can trigger self-reinforcing feedback loops to the upside, especially when the reflexive impacts of hedging activities by option market markers are taken into account. In this sense, market mechanics triggered by the S&P 500 inclusion and further fueled by Trump's campaign seem to be another factor leading to the perfect storm to the upside we have witnessed over the last few months.

In today's market ecosystem, feedback loops have become increasingly important in understanding the behaviour of market prices. The short and gamma squeezes on Tesla following the pandemic is another example. On top, Tesla's share price was also fueled by its inclusion in the S&P 500 in December 2020. Those three dynamics were self-reinforcing, and the consequence was a historic rally in the share price. Even though there was a large crowd of fundamental investors screaming about overvaluation, it did not matter to market mechanics. MicroStrategy: Feedback Loops Also Work In Reverse

Valuation

As disclosed in the Q4-24 conference call, Palantir expects revenues of ~$3.75 billion for the full year 2025, the equivalent of a ~30% growth rate compared to the $2.87 billion reported in 2024. As indicated in the chart below, Palantir's revenue growth rate has been accelerating since the second quarter of 2023. For example, the revenue growth of 36.0% reported in the fourth quarter represents a drastic acceleration versus the 19.6% growth reported in Q4-23. Using the Q1-25 revenue guidance of $860 million provided by management, Palantir could repeat the impressive financial results delivered in Q4-24. In my view, accelerating growth is another key element in understanding the recent pop in the share price. To justify the current valuation, revenue growth must continue to increase. In fact, simply maintaining a 30% revenue growth rate over multiple years would be insufficient to support the current market capitalization.

Using the growth curve illustrated below, it would take Palantir slightly more than 11 years to generate revenues equivalent to its current ~$275 billion market capitalization. Using a simple price-to-sales ratio based on 2024 revenues, Palantir currently trades at more than 95x revenues, one of the highest multiples I have ever seen for a company of this size. Using the 2025 revenue guidance of $3.75 billion, we are talking a price-to-sales multiple of ~73x. Scott McNealy, the former CEO of Sun Microsystems, made history with his speech regarding stocks trading at 10x revenues during the dot-com bubble. Although I fully recognize Palantir's massive growth opportunities, the current AI boom seems to be producing companies trading at multiple that Scott McNealy could probably have never imagined.

At 10 times revenues, to give you a 10-year payback, I have to pay you 100% of revenues for 10 straight years in dividends. That assumes I can get that by my shareholders. That assumes I have zero cost of goods sold, which is very hard for a computer company. That assumes zero expenses, which is really hard with 39,000 employees. That assumes I pay no taxes, which is very hard. And that assumes you pay no taxes on your dividends, which is kind of illegal. And that assumes with zero R&D for the next 10 years, I can maintain the current revenue run rate. Now, having done that, would any of you like to buy my stock at $64? Do you realize how ridiculous those basic assumptions are? You don’t need any transparency. You don’t need any footnotes. What were you thinking? Scott McNealy, Sun Microsystems

In my forecast, I used stable gross margins of 80%, which are in line with what Palantir reported over the last three years. Could gross margins increase further over time? It is possible, but 80% gross margins are some of the highest I am aware of, and maintaining those levels over the long run would be an achievement in itself.

Since 2019, Palantir has made significant progress in amortizing sales and marketing expenses, as well as general and administrative expenses. Once combined, those two line items represented 103.8% of revenues in 2019 compared to 51.7% in 2024. This is where the power of operating leverage comes into play. The chart below illustrates the long-term assumption used in my model. A more optimistic scenario would assume a faster amortization, mostly as a consequence of faster revenue growth. Given Palantir's current position in its lifecycle, imposing strict control on operating expenses may not be the ideal strategy if it limits growth.

Over the last three years, Palantir reported research and development expenses representing approximately 18% of revenue. In my model, I assumed a gradual amortization of these expenses until they reached 5.0% of revenues in 2041. Of course, nobody knows what the true figures will look like, but the key point is that Palantir's free cash flow is likely to increase quite dramatically in the coming years as the company has now reached a critical size.

Using the assumptions discussed above, it would take almost 15 years for Palantir to generate operating income equivalent to its current market capitalization of $275 billion. Market participants buying Palantir today are making implicit cash outlays of $275 billion in exchange for being entitled to the operating income moving forward. Under this scenario, an investor paying today's valuation would simply have recouped its capital by 2040; no return on capital would be earned yet.

If we include a return on capital of 10%, it would take a holding period of 22 years for Palantir to generate operating income equivalent to its current market capitalization. Of course, this is entirely dependent on the assumptions mentioned above. However, getting the thinking right is much more important than getting the various numbers precisely right. Those calculations reflect what the business could potentially earn on its own, irrespective of what is happening surrounding financial markets.

Considering the massive duration embedded in Palantir's $275 billion market capitalization, many things can go wrong before an investor can recoup his capital intrinsically through the business's operation and growth. Evidently, almost nobody would actually own the stock for such a long period of time, meaning that people buying the stock today are entirely dependent on finding another buyer at a higher price. Simply being patient and getting out of a hole through the intrinsic growth of the business would be vastly insufficient if market sentiment were to change.

Conclusion

Over the last few hours, the Trump administration ordered the Pentagon to develop plans to reduce defence spending by 8% in each of the next five years. Considering the leading-edge nature of Palantir's products, I doubt the company would be impacted by a reduction in defence spending, but the impact on market sentiment has been immediate.

Just a few hours earlier, Alex Karp also announced plans to sell $1.2 billion in common stock. According to Barron's, Palantir's CEO sold 40.7 million shares in 2024 at an average price of $47.99 per share. It is another element that could indicate the beginning of a sentiment shift. For the stock price to become under pressure, a mere reduction in euphoria would be sufficient enough.

Using the assumptions detailed in this article, investors buying the stock today would have to wait ~22 years before earning an annual return on capital of 10%. An extremely long payback period makes the stock particularly vulnerable to a shift in sentiment.

For disclosure purposes, I have a long position in the Direxion Daily PLTR Bear 1X Shares (PLTD) ETF. Although inverse ETFs are subject to beta slippage, they provide maximum downside risk in return due to their daily rebalancing mechanism. As described in my previous short articles about MicroStrategy and Tesla, extreme prudence is required in this kind of situation. I have now closed profitably my short position on Tesla and MicroStrategy. This capital has been redeployed to a short Palantir position via PLTD.

data.title: Uniti Group Surges As The Bullish Case Becomes Obvious
data.description:
February 21st ended up being a really exciting day for shareholders of Uniti Group Inc. (NASDAQ:UNIT). At one point, shares of the company were up as much as 10.3%. This came after news that the company reported financial results for the final quarter of its 2024 fiscal year and offered up guidance for 2025. Considering that it's one of my largest holdings at this time, it stands to reason that I was a very happy camper with this development. But even with this surge, I maintain that shares are drastically undervalued. Even though the company fell short of analysts’ expectations, the overall picture for the business is positive, and I do believe that the company deserves the “strong buy” rating that I have had for some time.

So far, my bullish stance on the company has played out well. Since I last reaffirmed it as a “strong buy” candidate back in October of last year, shares are up a whopping 14.7%. That's well above the 6.3% upside achieved by the S&P 500 (SP500) over the same window of time. But that's nothing compared to performance over a longer window of time. Since I first upgraded the company to a “strong buy” in May 2024, shares have achieved an upside of 40.3%. That's massively above the 18.3% upside achieved by the broader market. But even with this kind of return, I maintain that further upside is appropriate. Because of this, I have decided to reaffirm the company as a ‘strong buy’ at this time.

The picture still looks strong

To be honest with you, the final quarter of the 2024 fiscal year was a bit mixed for Uniti Group. The business reported revenue of $293.3 million. That represents an increase of 2.7% compared to the $285.7 million reported the previous year. Having said that, revenue was still $2.3 million lower than what analysts were hoping to see. Digging into the data further, we can see that most of this upside came from its Uniti Leasing business, with sales up 3.1% from $214.9 million to $221.7 million. The overwhelming majority of this revenue comes from the firm’s relationship with Windstream. And that party is subject to small annual escalations in how much it's paying. Meanwhile, Uniti Fiber, which focuses on infrastructure solutions like cell site backhaul and small cell for wireless operators, not to mention other types of customers, experienced a 1.3% increase in revenue from $70.7 million to $71.7 million.

Even though revenue increased during this time, profitability actually worsened. Earnings per share contracted from $0.13 to $0.09, even missing analysts’ estimates by $0.01. This translated to a decline in net profits from $3.4 million to $21 million. Some of this was because of a smaller income tax benefit. However, the company also saw a surge in transaction-related costs from $2.8 million to $7.7 million. FFO (Funds from Operations) per share ended up falling slightly from $0.32 to $0.30, causing overall FFO to decline from $85.7 million to $77.9 million. But on an adjusted basis, it rose from $0.34 to $0.35. This still ended up being $0.01 lower than what analysts were hoping to see. However, it did translate to an increase in adjusted FFO from $91.6 million to $92 million.

The good news is that operating cash flow jumped from $162.5 million to $185.6 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we get a more modest increase from $102.5 million to $103.7 million. Meanwhile, EBITDA for the company grew from $231.1 million to $239.5 million. To be honest with you, I consider these three cash flow metrics to be more important than net profits, FFO, or adjusted FFO.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for 2024 as a whole relative to 2023. Except for adjusted operating cash flow and adjusted FFO, the picture for the business improved across the board. Management expects further improvements for 2025. They currently believe that revenue will be between just under $1.20 billion and $1.22 billion. At the midpoint, this would represent an increase of 3.4% compared to the $1.17 billion the company reported for 2024. Net profits attributable to common shareholders should rise to between $95 million and $115 million. Even the low end of this is higher than the $91.3 million the company reported for 2024.

When it comes to guidance, management was actually quite detailed. In the image above, you can even see certain per-share estimates that the company has provided. But focusing on the big picture, we can also expect an FFO of between $322 million and $342 million compared to the $294.5 million reported for 2024 and an adjusted FFO of between $369 million and $389 million. That figure would be above the $358.9 million reported for last year. Lastly, EBITDA for the company should come in somewhere between $966 million and $986 million. For context, for 2024, that figure was only $940.1 million.

All things considered, the picture for the business is looking up. In addition to continuing to grow, the company is also making progress elsewhere. For instance, from the third quarter to the fourth quarter, management paid down $127.2 million worth of net debt. After the end of the quarter, the company ended up closing a $589 million note offering secured by some of the company's fiber assets. These carry with them a 6.5% weighted average interest rate. With the proceeds, the firm paid down $125 million of senior notes due in 2028 which had an annual interest rate of 10.50%. They also paid off $275 million from an asset-based credit facility that had a 10.33% interest rate.

We don't know what the company is planning to do with the remaining $189 million. I hope that they will pay off other debt as well. However, even though they only mentioned what they did with $400 million of the proceeds, the interest savings on that amounts to $38.3 million per year compared to the $41.5 million in interest expense the debt they paid off cost shareholders.

I also think it would be appropriate, at this time, to talk about its pending merger with Windstream. It was news of the two companies combining that caused me to upgrade the stock to a ‘strong buy’ even though shares initially plunged on the news. Every day, this decision looks a bit better than the day before. And that's difficult considering how good the development was in my view.

On February 20th, updated financial data for Windstream became public. Year over year, the company claimed that fiber coverage grew by 11%, resulting in 1.6 million consumer premises passed and with 37% of fiber coverage now in the firm’s Kinetic markets. The enterprise also grew fiber subscribers by 16% year over year. This brought the total number of fiber subscribers up to 446,000. The overall penetration rate for these customers is 27.4%. That compares favorably to the 26.3% that the company enjoyed the previous year. Furthermore, fiber subscriber revenue jumped from $321 million in 2023 to $408 million in 2024, driven in part by a rise in monthly fiber ARPU from $68.74 to $71.07.

Another positive thing about the company is that the market potential is significant. Management seems particularly interested in AI infrastructure. In the image below, you can see the magnitude of this market and how much it is slated to grow by the year 2030. Naturally, this offers the business a meaningful upside as it continues to make significant investments in growing its network.

For 2025, Windstream expects to grow the number of premises passed to roughly 2 million. And the number of consumer fiber subscribers is forecasted to hit around 550,000, driven not only by the network expansion, but also by an increase in the fiber penetration rate to 28%. Overall profitability for the company will probably remain largely unchanged from 2024 to 2025. For instance, EBITDAR (which is EBITDA before factoring out rents paid to Uniti Group) hit $1.46 billion in 2024. But for 2025, it is expected to be $1.4 billion. Actual EBITDA for the company was just under $1.01 billion last year. Using the data provided by management, which is only partial since Windstream is not a publicly traded company and does not need to provide every detail, I estimated that the adjusted operating cash flow was around $801 million.

In the table above, you can see certain projections regarding what profitability and valuations look like for both Uniti Group and Windstream based on these updated financial results. On an EV-to-EBITDA basis, Uniti Group is trading at a multiple of only 7.3. For Windstream, that number is 4.5. And on a combined basis, the multiple should be around 7.3 using estimates for 2025.

In the table below, you can see, using previous projections by management for what the combined company will look like by the year 2028, how the company should be valued. Even in the most conservative case provided by management, we should be looking at a fair value per share for the combined business of $12.28. Compared to the $5.94 that shares of Uniti Group are going for currently, this would mean an annualized upside of 19.9%. But using some of the more aggressive scenarios, we would be looking at an upside of as much as 51.5% per annum. This could take shares up to as much as $31.29.

Takeaway

Based on the data provided, I must say that I am pleased with how things are turning out. Even though it is one of my largest holdings, I honestly regret not buying more of it. Yes, management did fall short of expectations when it came to the top and bottom lines for the quarter. But the overall picture for shareholders looks positive, and I see no reason why, given the massive amount of upside that's on the table, that shares should be rated anything besides a “strong buy.”

data.title: HYLD:CA: An Ideal Mix Of Income And Capital Appreciation
data.description:
As a retail investor, it's often hard to decide what's more important - a yield you can take advantage of immediately or an asset that keeps appreciating over time. There's ample risk (not to mention tax considerations) with both approaches, of course, especially if you're on the aggressive side of the risk divide. With income-generating assets, there are hard cash flows that you can discount to arrive at a fair value, but your assumptions may be way off. Even more complex is identifying growth assets that will inherently appreciate over time and still give you a solid real (as opposed to nominal) return that beats the market - or doesn't.

What if you had one product that gave you the best of both worlds - a yield in the double digit percentages and a supporting price return that's enough to boost the total return to over what you'd get had you invested in a broader index? You'd naturally jump at it, because it helps spread your risk across income-generating as well as growth-oriented assets. The second thing it does is give you a regular payout that you can either reinvest into the asset to give you those above-par total returns or add to your personal monthly cash flow to take care of life's expenses.

Introducing an Ideal Mix of Income and Capital Appreciation

One such opportunity I'd like to present to you today is the TSX-listed Hamilton Enhanced U.S. Covered Call ETF (TSX:HYLD:CA). The fund comes with 0% management fee (for now, at least) and was incepted a little over three years ago in February 2022, so there's ample data for our research and analysis purposes.

Now, normally, you'd see significant NAV erosion in a covered call play because you're capping the upside to your strike price, and there's little to no downside protection so your asset base might depreciate a lot faster than you expect. Besides, there's also a Catch-22-type situation here, in that highly volatile assets often yield better results in terms of premiums collected, but it can also mean that your underlying assets frequently get called away or, worse, perform dismally to the downside with no protection in place.

Hamilton navigates this complex set of risks by investing in its own high-yield ETFs, which generally come with a lower risk profile than a basket of stocks due to the effect of diversification. It also uses a very reasonable 25% leverage on its cash (cash borrowings, essentially) to give you a better distribution every month that you can then reinvest into HYLD:CA. The fund does not engage in covered calls directly but benefits from this strategy being used by the other Hamilton ETFs that it holds. The high level of diversification is maintained by periodically rebalancing the ETF's underlying assets, but I find the turnover of 115% to be much higher than I'd like, and that's because of the greater transaction and other costs involved.

Despite these costs, the fund has a trailing yield of around 12% and a 3Y return of nearly 30%, allowing it to beat the TSX 60 by a significant 750bps margin on a total return basis.

To give you some added perspective, the fund has not outperformed the S&P 500 Index (SP500) on a 3Y total return basis but has shown superior results over the past year.

Underlying Holdings Driving Total Return

As I mentioned, the fund only invests in other ETFs from Hamilton, but essentially uses a somewhat similar strategy of allocations to different sectors.

To help capture the strong momentum of the S&P 500, the fund invests about a third of its $834 million AUM in the Hamilton U.S. Equity YIELD MAXIMIZER ETF (SMAX:CA). This is a roughly equal-weighted collection of 47 stocks from the mega-cap and large-cap segments that is ultimately still tech-heavy but otherwise well-diversified.

Most of the remaining assets are in the form of sector-specific ETFs that help bring balance. It doubles down on technology by design because the broader holding is also heavily weighted to tech, but there's some balance from underlying ETFs that focus on a few key areas such as financials, healthcare, gold producers, and REITs. The rest of the cash is held as treasuries and bonds.

Since, as we've discussed, the underlying ETFs engage in covered call strategies of their own, HYLD:CA does very little of the heavy lifting on the income side. Instead, it piggybacks on the yields of these ETFs and debt securities, while using its cash leverage to further enhance its investment income.

Would I Recommend HYLD:CA?

Despite the U.S. market being grossly overvalued after the rise in aggregate P/E since October 2022, I'd still be comfortable recommending this ETF because it manages to capture the AI-centric momentum of the tech sector in the United States. The fund is also CAD-hedged to reduce currency fluctuation risks, which is good for U.S. investors. The dollar has seen some weakness of late, which is a headwind, but while that's a bit of a concern, it's not yet time to hit the panic button.

So yes, I would recommend a Buy for HYLD:CA, but with the caveat that there may be other funds that have even better price and total return profiles over the past three years.

I haven't covered any of these in the past, but it actually looks like there are several that have outperformed HYLD:CA over the preceding 3Y period. I could recommend them as better buys just on the total return alone, but that would be a mistake because I don't yet know the level of risk exposure on those ETFs. They did better before now, but there could be key catalysts that present strong headwinds, and other risks.

As an example, I could recommend the Vanguard S&P 500 Index ETF (VFV:CA) with its 3Y total return of over 63%, but I wouldn't know until I looked closer that it only holds one ETF, the Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO). That means more concentration risk, less diversification (although that's captured by VOO), and therefore a higher chance of underperforming HYLD:CA in a sudden downturn or even a sideways market, as shown below.

As such, I'm recommending HYLD:CA for now, with a promise to cover some of the other similar ones in the days to come.

data.title: 3 Biotech/Healthcare Names Under $10 I Am Buying Now
data.description:
I thought I would do something different in today's column. Instead, of a deep granular look at a biotech name, today I will highlight three biotech/healthcare names under $10 a share I currently find attractive on a longer-term basis. I have been accumulating larger positions in each name, via a combination of covered call orders and straight equity purchases, in recent trading sessions. All these small-cap names are down decently from recent highs, it should be noted.

Let's start with Ardelyx (ARDX) which I last did an in-depth piece here on Seeking Alpha in December. However, this small-cap biopharma concern just posted its Q4 numbers on Thursday. The company has one primary asset in tenapanor. The compound is known both as Ibsrela (FDA approved in 2019) for the treatment of irritable bowel syndrome with constipation (IBS-C). It is branded as Xphozah (FDA approved in October 2023) for control of serum phosphorus levels in adult patients with chronic kidney disease (CKD) on dialysis.

Ardelyx had GAAP earnings of two cents a share, in line with expectations. Revenues rose 238% on a year-over-year basis to just over $116 million, nearly $5 million north of the consensus. Ibsrela net product sales for the quarter were $53.8 million, up 32% sequentially from the third quarter. During the year, Ibsrela delivered $158.3 of net product sales. This beat management's guidance delivered with its third quarter press release of between $145 million to $150 million. Leadership provided initial FY2025 Ibsrela revenue guidance of between of $240 million to $250 million.

Xphozah did $57.2 million of net sales in the fourth quarter and produced $160.9 million of net sales in FY2024, its first full year of commercialization. As noted in my previous article, Ardelyx is locked in a fight with the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services [CMS] on how Xphozah will be reimbursed in 2025. That said, management did reiterate that it believes that XPHOZAH will achieve $750 million in annual U.S. net product sales revenue before its patent term expiration. Management also reaffirmed that it believes Xphozah and Ibsrela combined eventually will see $1.75 billion of peak sales.

That leaves a biopharma with a market cap of just north of $1.3 billion after Thursday's post earnings pullback, that is moving towards profitability and also has approximately $250 million of cash and marketable securities on its balance sheet. Given potential peak sales, that seems quite cheap.

Up next, is a company called Geron (GERN) that I posted a piece on back in November. After a long and winding road, Geron finally became a commercial concern when the FDA finally approved its primary asset imetelstat in June of last year. The compound is now better known by its brand names Rytelo, and is greenlighted for treating lower risk myelodysplastic syndromes.

Despite a promising market rollout of Rytelo, the shares are down some 40% from their post FDA approval highs. The stock currently trades just over $2.50 a share and sport an approximate market capitalization of $1.5 billion. Rytelo did $28.2 million of net product revenue in Q3. In mid-January, management disclosed preliminary Q4 Rytelo revenues of between $45 million to $46 million. Rytelo's approved indication presents a large market opportunity for Geron.

Rytelo should also be approved in Europe to treat lower risk myelodysplastic syndromes in the first half of 2025. In addition to its approved indication, the company is also pursuing development of Rytelo as both a monothery and as part of a combination therapy for other types of hematologic malignancies. The most advanced study is evaluating Rytelo as a monotherapy to treat relapsed/refractory MF. This Phase 3 trial should disclose an interim analysis early in 2026 and a full analysis in the first half of 2027.

As of early November, the company had just over $540 million of cash and marketable securities on its balance sheet against under $150 million in long-term debt and royalty obligations. So far in 2025, six analyst firms including Needham, Stifel Nicolaus and Barclays have either reiterated or assigned Buy ratings on the stock. Price targets proffered range from $3.50 to $10 a share, with a median price target of just over seven bucks a share. Given Rytelo's potential peak sales, the stock does appear to be cheap.

Finally, we have Personalis, Inc. (PSNL) which I last fully covered in late October here in late October. Technically, the company is not a biotech company but operates in a similar niche providing oncology tests and analytics. More importantly, the stock is down from its highs at the start of this year, despite some positives since I last touched on this small-cap name.

The stock currently trades around $5.50 a share and sports an approximate market capitalization of just south of $400 million. As a reminder, this company offers advanced cancer genomic tests and analytics to primarily large Enterprise and Research. The company's developmental platform can create testing that provides more comprehensive molecular data about each patient's cancer and immune response. This helps their customers better develop their own products.

In December, Personalis announced an extension to its agreement with Moderna (MRNA) around its utilization of Personalis’s ImmunoID NeXT Platform. More importantly, management disclosed a $50 million investment by Merck (MRK) in equity via private placement. This will bring up the drug giant's stake in Personalis to just north of 16%. This follows a $36 million equity investment by Tempus AI, Inc (TEM) in summer last year.

Then on January 7th, leadership posted preliminary FY2024 sales guidance of $84.6 million. This would be up 15% on a year-over-year basis and just over $1 million above the analyst firm consensus at the time. The company is still unprofitable but is cutting losses significantly. After a loss of $2.25 a share in FY2023, PSNL is trending to a loss of just under $1.50 a share in FY2024 and the current analyst consensus sees the red ink falling further to $1.08 a share in FY2025.

In addition to recent equity investment by Merck, the company ended its most recent quarter with nearly $145 million worth of cash and marketable securities on its balance sheet. Leadership guided cash usage for FY2024 at the time of between $53 million to $55 million. So far in 2025, a half dozen analyst firms including Needham and TD Cowen have reissued Buy ratings on PSNL. Price targets proffered range from $7.25 to $9.00 a share.

Given profitability is several years down the line, PSNL is a small speculative buy that I am holding exclusively via covered call positions. This provides significant downside protection while I slowly accumulate a stake in this small-cap name.

data.title: Aurinia Pharmaceuticals: There's Still Time For Sales Growth
data.description:
Aurinia Pharmaceuticals Inc.'s (NASDAQ:AUPH) sole approved product, Lupkynis, for the treatment of lupus nephritis, didn't produce strong product revenue growth going from Q2'24 to Q3'24. The stock is now approaching Q4'24 earnings and a resumption of sales growth could again produce a rally in the stock. When I last covered the name in September 2024, I rated it a buy on a potential near-term milestone for Japanese approval of Lupkynis, the start of royalty payments from Japanese sales and underlying growth in terms of patients on Lupkynis therapy. This article takes a look at competitor developments, upcoming earnings and a potential trade strategy.

Renewed sales growth could produce a rally

Even though AUPH's Q3'24 earnings were met with a rally, there was still one issue, modest net product revenue growth for Lupkynis. While AUPH reported $55.0M in net product revenue in Q2'24, net product revenue in Q3'24 was $55.5M.

Total net revenues of $67.8M for Q3'24 were aided by a $10M milestone thanks to Japanese approval of Lupkynis, and AUPH reported a net income for the quarter of $14.4M. Still, underlying revenue growth would be ideal. Nonetheless, there have been times when AUPH's net product revenues seemed to stagnate (such as the period Q2'23-Q4'23) and renewed growth was met with a rally. The recent pullback then could represent another buying opportunity, and that is certainly part of my current thesis.

Q4'24 earnings preview

AUPH has announced it will report Q4'24 and full year earnings on February 27, 2025. With Q3'24 earnings, AUPH provided guidance for full-year 2024 net product revenues of $210M-$220M. The company has already reported net product revenues of $158.6M in the first three quarters of 2024, so another quarter of ~$55M would already bring AUPH to ~$214M for the year. The company seems unlikely to miss that revenue guidance then, since Q4 has tended to outperform Q3, even if only by a little. I think full year net product revenues of $215M are achievable.

AUPH also announced further restructuring with Q3'24 earnings (estimated to save $40M+ annually), but that is going to produce a one-time charge of $15M-$19M according to AUPH. With total cost of sales and operating expenses of $56M in Q3'24, a Q4'24 number of $70M-$75M seems possible when adding in restructuring expenses. That one-time charge could thus wipe out the company's net income for the quarter, leaving it perhaps even with a narrow net loss.

AUPH had cash, cash equivalents, restricted cash and investments of $348.7M as of September 30, 2024, up from $330M at the end of Q2'24. I expect little change in the current cash balance this time around due to the one-time restructuring charge (not all of that may be cash, but AUPH hasn't guided one way or another on that).

AUPH had 143,179,269 shares of common stock outstanding as of November 6, 2024, AUPH has a market cap of $1.12B ($7.83 per share).

Update on competition

In February, results from a phase 3 trial of Roche Holding AG's (OTCQX:RHHBY) obinutuzumab (Gazyva) in lupus nephritis were published in an article in The New England Journal of Medicine. Gazyva did outperform the standard of care in terms of producing a complete renal response, but it is not yet approved for marketing in that indication. In any case, results were announced in a press release in September 2024, prior to the recent publication, and so the market has known about this potential competitor for months.

It is also worth monitoring developments in systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) since disease control in these patients could also reduce the number of patients developing lupus nephritis, and thus reduce the number of patients who might use Lupkynis. In that regard, UCB and Biogen Inc. (BIIB) announced in November 2024 that dapirolizumab pegol had met the primary endpoint in a phase 3 trial in SLE. A second phase 3 trial of dapirolizumab, PHOENYCS FLY is underway, but won't be completed until early 2027.

Trade idea, rating, and risks

AUPH is off the recent highs despite the company reporting a net income last quarter and initially making gains in the month following Q3'24 earnings. I suspect Q4'24 earnings are likely to come with confirmation of continued revenue growth, and the company is also expected to report some initial clinical data from AUR200 in H1'25. AUPH then isn't devoid of chances for positive updates, and RHHBY's drug could be a competitor. However, I think there is time for at least one more rally in AUPH's stock prior to competitors causing problems for the company. Given these factors, I currently rate AUPH a buy.

A first risk facing any investor going long AUPH is that if Q1'25 earnings do not come with a confirmation of continued product revenue growth, then the stock could trade down further.

Secondly, there is a risk that AUR200 doesn't look promising, even though initial data is simply from a single-ascending dose study, the stock could sell off.

Lastly, even though AUPH is restructuring, the company could look at those cash savings, and its existing cash pile, and acquire another drug to add to its pipeline, to reduce its dependence on Lupkynis. Even if AUPH can acquire something at or close to the commercial stage, the market might have mixed feelings about the prospects of that asset, and the stock could fall.

data.title: IUS: Confirming Excellence In The Large Value Category
data.description:
This article updates my review of IUS published in April 2024 in light of current holdings and recent performance.

IUS Strategy

Invesco RAFI™ Strategic US ETF (NASDAQ:IUS) was launched on 09/12/2018 and tracks the Invesco Strategic US Index. It has 552 holdings, a 30-day SEC yield of 1.52% and an expense ratio of 0.19%. Dividends are paid quarterly.

As described by Invesco, US-listed companies are assigned a business size score and a quality score. The business size score is based on the equal-weight average of four factors: sales, operating cash flow, total return of capital (dividends and buybacks) and book value. For the real estate sector, operating cash flow is replaced by FFO (funds from operations) and book value by total assets. The quality score is based on the equal-weight average of two factors: sales-to-assets ratio (trailing 12 months) and its 5-year growth. The underlying index includes companies ranked in the top 90% by business size score and in the top 80% by quality score. Constituents are weighted based on float-adjusted business size scores. The index is reconstituted annually. The portfolio turnover rate was 14% in the most recent fiscal year. This article will use as a benchmark the S&P 500 (SP500), represented by SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY).

IUS Portfolio

Large and mega-cap companies represent about 67% of asset value. IUS has significant exposure in information technology (18.5%). Nonetheless, it downplays this sector compared to the S&P 500, as well as financials. On the other hand, it overweights mostly energy. Consequently, IUS has a more balanced sector breakdown than the benchmark.

The portfolio is quite diversified and risks related to individual companies are low to moderate. Indeed, the top 10 holdings, listed in the next table, represent 26.2% of asset value and the heaviest position weighs about 5%.

IUS looks equivalent to SPY in performance since inception, but it has lagged the benchmark by about 5% over the last three years. Nonetheless, it was more resilient in the 2022 market downturn, as plotted on the chart below.

The annual sum of distribution has increased by 53% in five years, from $0.49 per share in 2019 to $0.75 in 2024. This dividend growth rate has greatly outpaced the cumulative inflation (about 23% at the same time, based on CPI).

Competitors

IUS is classified in the “Large Value” category, so it makes sense to compare it with ETFs in the same category. The next table lists characteristics of IUS and five large-cap value ETFs with at least six years of track record:

IUS is the smallest fund of this group in assets under management, it has the lowest yield, but the highest dividend growth rate. The next chart compares total returns since its inception. IUS comes in first position, about 23% ahead of the second-best performer FVAL.

Takeaway

Invesco RAFI™ Strategic US ETF has a portfolio of over 500 stocks selected and weighted based on a fundamental size score and a quality score. It is more diversified in sectors and holdings than the S&P 500 index and has solid value characteristics. Moreover, it is almost on par with the benchmark since its inception (while most value funds have been lagging far behind it) and has outperformed popular large-cap value funds in return and dividend growth. In conclusion, IUS keeps its position among the best large-cap value funds.

data.title: Is EPR Properties A Safe Long-Term Dividend Choice?
data.description:
2025: Prime Time for U.S. Dividends

From slowing economic growth to a hawkish Fed to Trump tariffs and geopolitical instability, preparing your portfolio for the unknown is paramount. But with so much uncertainty in the air, it can be hard to know where to begin. Stocks with solid dividend growth offer investors a two-for-one reliable income stream and protection against potential inflation and market volatility.

Even though the market has been up and down recently, history shows us one thing is for certain: it’s a great time to be a dividend investor. In 1980, total dividends paid in the United States were just $100B. Since then, annual growth has averaged about 8% per year, bringing the figure to $2.8 trillion in 2023, up $1.5T since 2008. That growth may have slowed in recent years, but the long-term trend remains higher.

The Fed’s cautious approach to lowering interest rates has also come as a favorable shift for real estate investment trusts, which benefit from a lower-rate environment due to reduced borrowing costs and more attractive dividend yields relative to bonds. REITs and real estate experienced some volatility during the higher-rate environment seen in 2023 and the beginning of 2024. But those with strong fundamentals and solid AFFO and FFO growth have proven to be a great option for upside in the current macroeconomic environment thanks to their attractive blend of growth and value characteristics. Earlier this year, I brought you my top REITs to buy in 2025: Getty Realty Corp. (GTY) and VICI Properties Inc. (VICI).In this piece, examine the quantitative attributes and dividend safety metrics of one more attractively valued, high-growth REIT: EPR Properties (EPR).

EPR Properties (NYSE: EPR)

  • Market Capitalization: $3.73B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 2/20/25): 10 out of 174

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 2/20/25): 2 out of 11

  • Dividend Yield (FWD): 6.95%

  • P/AFFO (FWD): 10.62

Ranking in the top 10 of Real Estate Stocks and 2out of 11 among Other Specialized REITs, EPR Properties (NYSE:EPR) is a triple-net lease REIT with over 352 properties and over 200 tenants across the U.S. and Canada focused on “experiential properties.” Its sprawling $6.9B portfolio includes cinemas, fitness destinations, ski resorts, zoos, amusement parks, concert stadiums, casinos, museums, sports complexes, private schools, and early childhood education centers. From various Topgolf and AMC Theatres to iFLY indoor skydiving simulators, EPR’s diverse portfolio has allowed it to capture entertainment-seeking consumers of all ages and interests.

That bet on in-person experiences backfired during the COVID-19 pandemic, as the REIT lost nearly 75% of its market value between February 10 and March 16, 2020. Additionally, one of its largest tenants, Regal Cinemas’s parent company, Cineworld, filed for bankruptcy. EPR’s current share price of $49.11 remains 30% below its early 2020 high. However, a box office renaissance (36% of EPR’s portfolio is concentrated on movie theatres) and a return of the “experience economy” have helped EPR shares bounce back 15.6% over the past year and 11% year-to-date.

While cinemas have yet to recover fully, the remainder of EPR's portfolio is in great shape as demand for in-person activities rebounds. Rent coverage surpasses even that of pre-Covid levels. EPR continues its strategy of diversifying away from movie theaters and investing more heavily in experiential properties like health and wellness retreats to kickstart future growth.

“Our investment strategy remains on track as we continue recycling proceeds from the sale of non-core assets and use free cash flow to invest in diversified experiential assets,” CEO Greg Silvers stated on its latest earnings call, calling out climbing gyms, hot springs, and Miraval spa hotels as key areas of expansion.

EPR Stock Performance and Dividend

EPR’s steady recovery could spark interest for growth-oriented investors given its strong performance and Quant Factor Grades. EPR Properties’ FFO of $1.29 beat expectations by $0.02 in Q3 2024. And even though the property group reported Y/Y declines in terms of both FFO and AFFO of 11.6% and 12.2%, respectively. SA analyst Jack Elias notes:

“The TTM figures are up compared to the figures from FY2020-FY2022 which shows that EPR is on the path back to pre pandemic operational levels. Additionally, there were no out of period deferral collections recognized in this quarter. By subtracting this figure from the Q3 FY23 FFO figure, FFO actually increased YoY.”

Post-pandemic box office recovery as pent-up demand for travel and in-person activities has aided the stock’s momentum, lifting its grade in the category from a C- to an A, with a powerful 3M price performance of 10.17% at a -5.2% difference to the sector median.

EPR’s upward trajectory is reflected by A+ 3Y AFFO growth of 40.3%, an 888.5% difference from the 4% sector median, as well as A+ 3Y dividend growth (CAGR) of 24.9%, a 567.5% difference to the 3.7% sector median. A solid AFFO payout ratio (FWD) of 70.9%, AFFO payout ratio (TTM) of 71.5%, trailing twelve-month AFFO/total revenue (TTM) of 51.2%, and AFFO yield (TTM) of 9.74% underline solid profitability, in addition to FFO/total revenue of 51.2% and an FFO yield of 9.9%, all indicating EPR's ability to pay out significant amounts to shareholders.

In the current macroeconomic environment, however, EPR’s near7% juicy dividend yield and ability to provide steady income and potentially hedge against inflation make it the most attractive in the near term. The surging dividend yield has helped lift EPR’s Valuation Grade to an A-, along with a trailing twelve-month return P/AFFO (TTM) of 10.27 (a -29.9% difference, and a P/AFFO (FWD) of 10.21 (a -34.5% difference) among other highly-rated metrics.

The company's Funds From Operations guidance was updated to $4.80-$4.92 per share for FY 2024, up e of $4.76 to $4.96, giving its annual dividend a 70% payout ratio. Meanwhile, its P/FFO (FWD) ratio of 10.08x reflects that it is significantly discounted to its peers at a -25.6% difference from the sector median. As EPR continues to shed theatre properties and build upon its wellness strategy, that valuation gap could lower, making its current high yield a deal worth pouncing on sooner rather than later. The company declared a $0.285/share monthly dividend, in line with previously announced dividends, on February 14.

Examining its dividend further, EPR’s Dividend Safety Score falls in the average range with an overall C mark. Its cash dividend payout ratio (TTM) is 73.5%, a 15% difference from the 64% sector median, and its FFO/total revenue ratio is 55.91%, a 35.2% difference from the 41.3% sector median.

Its overall Dividend Yield Score is more highly rated at an overall A-, summarizing many of the positive attributes discussed above. EPR has demonstrated its ability to grow its dividends attractively for shareholder returns. Learn more about Quant Dividend Grades here.

Concluding Summary:

EPR’s post-pandemic momentum is gaining fast as consumers crave in-person experiences in the steadily recovering movie industry and in faster-growing verticals like wellness and outdoor activities. EPR offers an ideal mix of value, strong fundamentals, and a fairly reliable dividend that complements any portfolio in high and lower inflationary environments. Improving financials, powerful growth tailwinds, and a large dividend at a discounted price, all reiterate SA Quant’s Strong Buy rating on this stock. The stock featured here can help complement a portfolio. Alternatively, we have many high-yielding Top REITs to choose from, or you can consider exploring Alpha Picks if you would like access to Seeking Alpha Quant’s best monthly ideas from the hundreds of top quant Strong Buy-rated stocks.

data.title: Couchbase Looks To Agentic AI For Further Growth Opportunities
data.description:
Investment Outlook

Couchbase, Inc. (NASDAQ:BASE) is preparing to report its fiscal year-end financial results on February 25, 2025.

I previously wrote about BASE in September 2023 with a Hold outlook due to continued client demand headwinds.

The company hasn’t instituted any headcount reduction, leading to ongoing high operating losses, which will be penalized in a higher cost of capital environment.

My outlook remains on Hold for Couchbase, Inc. for the near term until we get greater visibility into its Agentic AI feature addition uptake and management's plans for cost optimization.

Couchbase’s Market And Approach

Couchbase operates in the Database-as-a-Service market and provides various NoSQL database configurations in a single subscription service-based platform, for applications such as analytics, transactional, mobile and AI requirements.

BASE recently introduced Agentic AI services for its Capella platform, which aims to help businesses automate as many functions as possible.

Per a MarketsandMarkets market research report, the worldwide cloud database and DBaaS market was an estimated $21.3 billion in 2023 and is expected to exceed $57 billion by 2028.

If achieved, this growth would represent a CAGR of an impressive 22% from 2024 to 2028.

The primary reasons for this expected growth are continuous demand growth for increasingly capable and easy-to-use cloud database systems along with new functionalities provided by vendors.

Also, the user base will continue to broaden with higher numbers of small and medium businesses using industry solutions.

Here is a chart illustrating expected growth trajectories by region through 2028 for the cloud database and DBaaS market:

Major industry participants include the following companies, so BASE faces intense competition from far larger players who can more effectively bundle their offerings and compete unfairly:

  • Oracle Corporation (ORCL).

  • Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN).

  • International Business Machines Corporation (IBM).

  • Microsoft Corporation (MSFT)

  • Google (GOOG) (GOOGL).

  • MongoDB, Inc. (MDB).

  • SAP SE (SAP).

  • Redis Labs.

  • EnterpriseDB.

  • Neo4j.

  • MariaDB.

  • DataStax.

  • MemSQL.

  • Others.

BASE goes to market with a “land and expand” sales and marketing focus through both its direct sales force and through ecosystem partners including integrators and resellers.

Recent Financial Trends

Total revenue by quarter (columns) has risen year-over-year due to rising recurring revenue, with its newer Capella platform accounting for over 15% of total ARR and one-third of its customers using the system. Operating income by quarter (red line) has remained significantly negative because of high SG&A and R&D expenses.

The degree of operating leverage has been trending lower (light red line) in recent quarters:

Gross profit margin by quarter (blue line) has trended lower as a result of an increasing business mix toward its Capella platform. Selling and G&A expenses as a percentage of total revenue by quarter (orange line) have fallen only recently but have remained far higher than typical SaaS companies. R&D expenses as a function of revenue (purple line) have trended marginally lower in recent quarters as the company continues to spend in this regard, but revenue run rates are higher.

Earnings per share (Diluted) have remained heavily negative, with only marginal improvement in recent quarters:

(All data in the above charts is GAAP).

For balance sheet results, BASE ended the quarter with $141.9 million in cash, equivalents, and short-term investments and zero debt.

Over the trailing twelve months, free cash used was ($30.5 million), and capital expenditures were $3.9 million, an unimpressive performance in this regard.

The company also paid a hefty $58.7 million in stock-based compensation in the last four quarters, so free cash used net of SBC was a whopping ($89.2 million).

SBC as a percentage of revenue over the trailing twelve-month period was a very high 28.7%.

In the past 12 months, BASE’s stock price has fallen 32% vs. that of the benchmark iShares Expanded Tech-Software Sector ETF's (IGV) rise of 23.1%, as the chart shows here.

Below is a handy major financial and operating metrics table showing forward estimates and trailing results:

Metric

Amount

EV/Sales - FWD

Seeking Alpha Quant Score

(Source — Seeking Alpha).

The company's Rule of 40 performance is shown in the table here and indicates BASE has made only marginal progress over several quarters, by reducing negative operating margin but at the cost of a lower topline revenue growth rate:

Rule of 40 Performance (Unadjusted)

FQ1 2024

FQ3 2025

Revenue Growth %

23.4%

19.3%

Operating Margin

-43.6%

-37.2%

Total

-20.2%

-17.9%

(Source — Seeking Alpha).

Why I’m Neutral On Couchbase

Couchbase is seeking to make the turn toward Agentic AI service support with recent upgrades to its Capella platform in that regard, as I’ve mentioned above.

However, it’s too early to tell if these represent meaningful additions that will improve the firm’s poor operating results.

While management may talk about ‘exercising expense discipline’, it has provided no evidence of that nor any specifics.

Other operating loss-making firms have instituted layoffs to reduce expenses, but have seen revenue decline as a result.

BASE has not taken this path, and it shows, with a higher revenue growth rate but continued heavy operating losses.

The problem with this approach is that in a relatively higher cost of capital environment, the market is penalizing heavy loss-making companies to a greater degree.

BASE’s stock price performance is suggestive of this situation, with sharp price drops associated with several recent earnings reports.

The graphic below illustrates the frequency of several negative-oriented keywords in the previous conference call, as a “sentiment indicator” of sorts:

The chart shows that management continues to blame “macroeconomic” “headwinds” and “challenges” for slow client demand for its products.

As to valuation, the stock market is valuing BASE at an EV/Sales multiple of around 4.0x on an estimated NTM revenue growth rate of 15.2% against a median Meritech Capital SaaS Index implied ARR growth rate of around 17% (Source).

Also, the benchmark index indicates a median Net Dollar Retention Rate of 110% as of December 2024, whereas BASE reports an NDR rate of 115%, exceeding the benchmark median.

The index of publicly held SaaS application software companies showed a forward EV/Revenue multiple median of around 6.3x on January 31, 2025, as the chart shows here:

So, by comparison, BASE is currently valued by the market at a material discount to the broader index as of January 31, 2025, despite a higher net retention rate and a decent expected forward revenue growth rate.

Management is seeing improved backlog, or RPO (Remaining Performance Obligations) suggesting support for a 15% forward growth rate.

As we await FQ4 2025’s report, I’ll be looking for management’s commentary on the uptake of its new Agentic AI features.

But problem areas will be the expected lower gross margin profile from its Capella platform and continued high operating losses, which leadership no doubt hopes to grow its way out of.

If it can’t produce strong growth figures, BASE will need to start cutting headcount, lest it suffers yet another selloff following a disappointing earnings report.

My outlook on BASE is Hold until we learn more from its upcoming fiscal year-end report.
